message:
perf(btree): split with_btree_op hot path from tracing; test(core): attached CTAS reads parent main source

crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs
----------------------------------
Hoists the tracing span + stats bookkeeping that `with_btree_op`
adds around every btree operation into a cold helper so the hot
path stays a straight-line call. The previous implementation
built a `tracing::span!` with a stringified `op_type` and
associated metadata before checking whether tracing was even
enabled; even with `tracing::enabled!` gating the later body, the
macro still paid for its inline metadata construction on every
call.

The new shape:

* `with_btree_op` is marked `#[inline(always)]` and, for the
  common case where
  `tracing::enabled!(target: "fsqlite.btree", Level::DEBUG)` is
  false, does:
    1. `instrumentation::record_operation(op_type)`.
    2. A direct `work(self)` call.
    3. A tail call to the new `finish_btree_op_postlude` helper.
  No span allocation, no stats wrapper, no closure layering.
* The traced branch is delegated to a new cold
  `with_btree_op_tracing` helper (also `#[inline(always)]` at its
  call site, but never inlined into the hot path because the
  early-return above peels it off).
* `finish_btree_op_postlude` is a tiny `#[inline(always)]` helper
  that runs `record_depth_gauge` and downgrades its error to a
  `debug!(...)` log with the `op_type.as_str()` tag, matching the
  old behaviour.

Net effect: fewer bytes in the hot `INSERT` / step instruction
stream, and no per-call `tracing::span!` metadata construction
when tracing is off. Matches the shape SQLite itself uses for
`sqlite3BtreeXxx`, where the instrumentation lives in a distinct
inline or cold helper.

crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs (test only)
--------------------------------------------------
New regression test
`test_create_table_as_select_in_attached_schema_reads_parent_main_source`
under `mod pager_routing_tests`. This is the end-to-end check that
locks in the CTAS-materialisation fix from the previous commit.

Test flow:

  1. Opens an in-memory origin connection and creates
     `main.src(id INTEGER, value TEXT)` with one row `(1, 'main')`.
  2. Attaches a file-backed database at a tempdir path as `aux`.
  3. Creates a *different* `aux.src(id INTEGER, value TEXT)`
     with a shadowing row `(1, 'aux')` — same table name, so any
     naive resolution that sends the SELECT through the attached
     connection will read the wrong row.
  4. Runs
     `CREATE TABLE aux.copy AS SELECT id, value FROM main.src;`.
  5. `SELECT id, value FROM aux.copy ORDER BY id;` must return
     `(1, 'main')`, not `(1, 'aux')`.

Before the materialisation fix this would either error (the old
strip-schema rewrite could not find `main.src` in the attached
connection) or return `(1, 'aux')` if the rewrite happened to
alias the source away. Now it correctly reads through the origin
connection and writes the materialised rows into the attached
schema.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
